title: Q1 Quiz 1: Unreal Setup, Interface, Navigation, and Transforms

questions.text: Which project template does the tutorial use to start from scratch?
choices: First Person
Blank
Vehicle
Photo Studio

questions.text: Why does the lesson keep the project set to Blueprint?
choices: Blueprint is required for screenshots.
The class is starting with visual scripting instead of C++.
Blueprint prevents starter content.
Blueprint changes the project location automatically.

questions.text: Which panel lists the objects that make up the current world or level?
choices: Details panel
Outliner
Content Drawer
Project Browser

questions.text: What must be held while using WASD to fly around the viewport?
choices: Left mouse button
Right mouse button
Shift only
Ctrl only

questions.text: Which shortcut changes the selected object to the scale gizmo?
choices: W
E
R
G

questions.text: Identify the tool or panel: The place where project folders and assets such as starter content props are browsed.
choices: Content Drawer or Content Browser
Outliner
Viewport
Toolbar

questions.text: Identify the shortcut: Press this key after selecting an object to focus the viewport camera on it.
choices: F
G
R
Q

questions.text: Identify the setting: This makes objects move, rotate, or scale in set increments.
choices: Game View
Snapping
World Settings
Starter Content

questions.text: Identify the view option: This hides editor widgets so the viewport shows what the player would see.
choices: Wireframe
Top View
Game View
Default Layout

questions.text: A student presses WASD but the viewport camera does not move. What should they check first?
choices: Whether the right mouse button is being held
Whether Starter Content is enabled
Whether the project is named correctly
Whether the Details panel is docked

questions.text: A student duplicated a statue with Ctrl+D but does not see a new statue. What probably happened?
choices: The duplicate is in the same location as the original
Ctrl+D deletes the original
The Outliner is hidden forever
The project changed from Blueprint to C++

questions.text: A student moved panels around and the editor layout is hard to use. What should they try?
choices: Window > Load Layout > Default Editor Layout
Delete the project folder
Turn on Wireframe mode
Disable all Show Flags

questions.text: Which sequence correctly describes the basic project setup workflow?
choices: Choose Games > Blank; set project name/location; confirm Blueprint and Starter Content; click Create
Click Create; choose Games > Blank; set project name/location; confirm Blueprint and Starter Content
Choose Vehicle; turn off Blueprint; click Create; rename the project later
Open Content Drawer; add objects; save map; choose project template

questions.text: Which sequence makes the most sense when building and documenting a simple scene?
choices: Add or duplicate an object; move it; rotate it; scale it; capture evidence
Capture evidence; scale it; choose a template; delete the object; save nothing
Rotate the camera; close Unreal; duplicate the project; move the monitor
Scale the scene first; import a texture; ignore placement; capture evidence

questions.text: Why is the Content Drawer or Content Browser important when building an Unreal project?
choices: It stores and organizes project assets such as folders, maps, meshes, props, and starter content.
It is only used to change the monitor brightness.
It replaces the viewport camera controls.
It automatically grades student screenshots.

title: Q1 Quiz 2 Draft: Post-Process, Materials, and Texture Maps

questions.text: What does auto exposure do in an Unreal scene?
choices: It automatically adjusts rendered brightness as the camera views darker or brighter areas.
It changes the size of every light in the level.
It deletes shadows so the scene renders faster.
It turns all materials into glowing materials.

questions.text: What is the main purpose of a Post Process Volume?
choices: To apply camera and image effects after the scene is rendered.
To permanently resize every mesh in the level.
To create a new project folder automatically.
To convert texture maps into 3D models.

questions.text: Why would you enable Infinite Extent Unbound on a Post Process Volume?
choices: To make the post-process settings affect the entire level.
To make the volume invisible in the Outliner.
To lock every object so it cannot move.
To make all texture maps import at full resolution.

questions.text: In the material graph, which input controls the main visible color of a surface?
choices: Base Color
Roughness
Normal
World Outliner

questions.text: Which sRGB setup is correct for a basic texture set?
choices: sRGB on for Base Color; sRGB off for Roughness and Normal maps.
sRGB off for every texture, including Base Color.
sRGB on for Roughness and Normal maps only.
sRGB only matters for mesh scale, not textures.

questions.text: Identify the term: a glow effect that appears around bright parts of the rendered image.
Identify the term: a shading or darkening effect around the edges of the image.
Identify the workflow term: a realistic material workflow that uses values such as metallic and roughness.
Identify the texture map: a map that fakes small bumps and surface direction changes so lighting responds with more detail.
A student changes exposure values in a Post Process Volume, but the scene does not look different in play mode. Name one thing they should check.
A student changes a material in the graph, including roughness and metallic values, but the object in the level still looks the same. What likely step did they miss?
A roughness map is imported, but the material behaves strangely and the grayscale information looks interpreted like color. What setting should the student check?
Put this post-process workflow in order: add a Post Process Volume, enable needed properties, make the volume affect the intended area, adjust exposure/effects, capture comparison screenshots.

Put this texture material workflow in order: import texture maps, check sRGB/compression settings, create a material, connect maps to inputs, adjust tiling with Texture Coordinate or Multiply, apply the material to a surface.
In 2-3 sentences, explain what a normal map adds to a material and why roughness or normal maps should usually have sRGB turned off.

title: Q1 Quiz 3 Draft: Master Materials, Static Meshes, Migration, and Lumen

questions.text: Why do master materials help a production workflow?
choices: They let creators reuse one parent setup while changing exposed values in instances.
They delete all material instances from the project.
They make imported meshes ignore scale.
They replace the need for lighting.

questions.text: What should you check soon after importing a static mesh?
choices: Scale, orientation, materials, and whether the asset appears correctly in the Content Drawer.
Only the color of the viewport grid.
Whether every light in the scene is static.
Whether the mesh has a quiz attached.

questions.text: Why is Unreal migration safer than copying a single visible asset file?
choices: Migration includes dependencies such as materials and textures.
Migration automatically creates a final screenshot.
Migration makes every asset a Blueprint.
Migration removes the need for a Content folder.

questions.text: What does global illumination describe in the Lumen lesson?
choices: Light bouncing around a scene and filling areas indirectly.
The keyboard shortcut for scaling objects.
The list of folders in a project.
A texture import setting for normal maps.

questions.text: Which evidence best proves a Lumen lighting improvement?
choices: Before and after screenshots from the same camera angle showing changed bounce light, shadow fill, or realism.
A screenshot of the login page.
A reflection that says "it looks better" with no visual evidence.
A screenshot with every light hidden.

questions.text: Identify the term: the original reusable material that controls the structure and available settings for its instances.
Identify the term: a related asset that another asset needs in order to work correctly.
Identify the term: an Unreal asset representing imported 3D geometry that can be placed in a level.
Identify the term: lighting that updates while lights or objects move instead of relying only on baked light maps.
A student creates a material instance but no useful controls appear in the instance. What should they check?
A migrated object appears in the destination project but its material is missing. What likely went wrong?
A lighting screenshot looks brighter after an exposure change, but the shadows still look flat. What should the student explain or test next?
Put this migration workflow in order: choose asset/folder, choose Migrate, review asset report, select destination Content folder, verify in destination project.
Put this lighting comparison workflow in order: frame a camera angle, capture before screenshot, adjust lighting/Lumen-related setting, capture after screenshot, write reflection.
In 2-3 sentences, explain why reusable material workflows and asset migration both help a studio work faster.

title: Q1 Quiz 4 Draft: Lighting, Landscapes, Foliage, and Mini-Worlds

questions.text: Which light type emits light in all directions from one point?
choices: Point light
Spotlight
Rectangle light
Landscape light

questions.text: Which light type is most like sunlight coming from far away?
choices: Directional light
Point light
Trigger volume
Foliage light

questions.text: What is the main purpose of the Smooth tool in Landscape mode?
choices: To soften harsh terrain bumps or sharp artifacts.
To delete all foliage from the map.
To turn every light into a skylight.
To export the project to Google Drive.

questions.text: Why should foliage density be adjusted instead of always left high?
choices: Too much foliage can hide the path, hurt readability, or affect performance.
Density only changes the color of the sky.
High density automatically creates Blueprints.
Low density deletes the landscape.

questions.text: What does a Blueprint preview help students understand in the mini-world lesson?
choices: How a scene could become interactive or playable.
How to replace Google Drive submissions.
How to make every mesh static.
How to remove all lighting from a level.

questions.text: Identify the term: a light that shines in one direction inside a cone shape.
Identify the term: the outdoor ground surface that can be sculpted into hills, valleys, and platforms.
Identify the term: the route a player is encouraged to follow through a space.
Identify the term: a visual scripting asset used to create behavior or interaction in Unreal.
A student lights a scene with three lights, but the viewer does not know where to look. What should they adjust?
A landscape has sharp spikes and rough artifacts after sculpting. What tool or workflow should the student use?
Trees painted with Foliage mode are leaning sideways on a slope. What setting should the student check?
Put this landscape workflow in order: create landscape, sculpt broad forms, flatten a usable area, smooth harsh edges, capture evidence.
Put this mini-world workflow in order: create terrain, add main path/focal point, paint foliage, adjust density/scale, play or camera test, submit evidence.
In 2-3 sentences, explain how lighting, terrain, and foliage work together to make a mini-world feel intentional.

title: Q3 Quiz 1 Draft: Production, Documentation, Planning, Setup, and Blockout

questions.text: What is the main purpose of a production log during the Q3 castle project?
choices: To track what was built, captured, changed, and planned next.
To replace all screenshots.
To store secret passwords.
To skip daily recording.

questions.text: Which evidence best supports a making-of documentary?
choices: A short screen recording showing a decision being made.
Only the final screenshot.
A blank project folder.
A title with no visual proof.

questions.text: Why should a castle environment have a focal point?
choices: It helps the viewer know what to notice first.
It hides all mistakes automatically.
It turns every mesh into a material.
It replaces player testing.

questions.text: Why is player scale important in a third-person castle project?
choices: It shows whether doors, paths, walls, and spaces feel believable from player view.
It changes the school calendar.
It makes screenshots unnecessary.
It deletes the need for terrain.

questions.text: Identify the term: supporting footage that helps explain a process, place, detail, or action.
Identify the term: a dated record of what was built, captured, changed, and planned next.
A student has a strong final screenshot but no process clips from the day. What should they do next class?
A castle looks impressive from editor view but too small when the player walks through it. What should the student check?
Put the daily Q3 evidence workflow in order: build, record, log, upload, submit.
In 2-3 sentences, explain why Q3 requires daily recording while students build the castle.

title: Q3 Quiz 2 Draft: Materials, Terrain Assets, Modular Kits, Composition, and Process Evidence

questions.text: What is the main job of landscape materials in a castle environment?
choices: To make terrain zones readable and believable.
To erase all landscape shapes.
To replace the production log.
To automatically build the castle.

questions.text: What makes an imported terrain asset support the scene?
choices: It matches scale, style, and story purpose.
It is random and large.
It hides the focal point.
It has no source note.

questions.text: Why are modular castle pieces useful?
choices: They allow repeated pieces to create larger structures efficiently.
They make all pieces unique automatically.
They prevent the need for saving.
They remove the need for composition.

questions.text: What does silhouette help communicate in a castle composition?
choices: The overall readable shape of the castle from a distance.
The exact file size.
The teacher password.
The audio level of a clip.

questions.text: Identify the term: reusable pieces assembled to build a larger structure.
Identify the term: the main visual shape or outline of an object or building.
A student downloads ten terrain assets but the scene looks cluttered and inconsistent. What should they do?
A modular castle wall repeats the same piece so obviously that it looks artificial. What can improve it?
Put the modular castle workflow in order: review kit, block silhouette, assemble pieces, add variation, record progress.
In 2-3 sentences, explain how material, asset, and modular choices can support the final documentary.

title: Q3 Quiz 3 Draft: Framing, Foliage, Storytelling, Lighting Mood, and Documentary Capture

questions.text: What is scene framing supposed to do?
choices: Guide the viewer toward important areas.
Hide all evidence.
Delete the player path.
Replace lighting.

questions.text: When can foliage hurt a scene?
choices: When it hides paths, focal points, or important silhouettes.
When it is placed intentionally.
When it creates scale.
When it supports mood.

questions.text: What is environmental storytelling?
choices: Using visual details to imply history, purpose, or events.
Writing a paragraph on every wall.
Using random props without meaning.
Turning off all lights.

questions.text: What is a lighting mood pass?
choices: Adjusting lighting choices to support a clear emotional tone.
Only making the scene brighter.
Deleting all post-process settings.
Submitting a folder with no screenshots.

questions.text: Identify the term: objects placed in the scene to suggest story, use, or history.
Identify the term: objects closest to the camera that can help frame the main subject.
A student paints dense trees directly over the player path. What should they adjust?
A castle has props everywhere but no clear story. What should the student do?
Put the lighting mood workflow in order: choose mood word, capture before, adjust lighting, capture after, explain the change.
In 2-3 sentences, explain how framing, foliage, story details, or lighting can make the castle documentary stronger.

title: Q3 Quiz 4 Draft: Post-Process, Walkthroughs, Documentary Evidence, and Portfolio Submission

questions.text: What is the goal of a cinematic screenshot?
choices: To present the scene with intentional framing, mood, and clarity.
To show random editor UI.
To avoid final evidence.
To replace all reflection writing.

questions.text: Why use post-process carefully?
choices: It can polish mood and image quality without hiding readability.
It should always make the scene blurry.
It deletes the need for lighting.
It prevents screenshots.

questions.text: What can a player walkthrough reveal that screenshots cannot?
choices: Pacing, route readability, collision issues, and how the scene feels in motion.
Only the file name.
The teacher gradebook.
Whether the Drive link is private.

questions.text: What should the final documentary prove?
choices: How the castle was made and improved over time.
Only that the final screenshot exists.
That no revisions were needed.
That the project has no files.

questions.text: Identify the term: a recorded player-view path through the environment.
Identify the term: a polished image chosen to represent the project in a portfolio or critique.
A student walkthrough moves too fast and misses the important castle details. What should they revise?
A final submission folder has great clips, but the teacher cannot open them. What is the likely issue and fix?
Put the final evidence workflow in order: select evidence, organize folder, check sharing, write reflection, submit link.
In 2-3 sentences, explain what evidence belongs in the final making-of documentary and why.

title: Q2 Quiz 1 Draft: Resolve Setup, Media, Timelines, and Rough Cuts

questions.text: What is the Project Manager used for?
choices: Creating, opening, backing up, and organizing Resolve projects.
Color correcting a single clip only.
Uploading videos directly to class rosters.
Recording camera audio on set.

questions.text: Which Resolve page is mainly used to import and organize footage?
choices: Media
Cut
Edit
Deliver

questions.text: What does the Media Pool contain?
choices: Clips, bins, timelines, and other media that live in the project.
Only the final exported movie.
Only the raw files on the desktop.
Only the teacher gradebook.

questions.text: Why are bins useful?
choices: They organize clips into meaningful project folders.
They automatically grade the final video.
They delete unused media from the computer.
They replace the timeline.

questions.text: What is audio sync?
choices: Matching separate audio and video so sound lines up correctly.
Making all audio silent.
Exporting the final file.
Changing clip color in the bin.

questions.text: What does the source viewer show?
choices: A clip before it is edited into the timeline.
Only the final exported video.
Only the class schedule.
A list of quiz scores.

questions.text: What does the timeline viewer show?
choices: The edited sequence at the current playhead position.
Only files that are not imported.
Only toolbar icons.
Only marker names.

questions.text: What is an in point?
choices: The selected start of the usable part of a source clip.
The final export folder.
The loudest part of an audio track.
The name of a Resolve page.

questions.text: What is an out point?
choices: The selected end of the usable part of a source clip.
The project backup file.
A timeline marker.
The timeline ruler.

questions.text: What is a rough cut?
choices: An early edit focused on order, timing, and story.
A final color-graded export only.
A folder of unorganized raw footage.
A paid Studio-only effect.

questions.text: Which choice best describes trimming?
choices: Removing or restoring clip edges to control timing.
Changing the school calendar.
Creating a new Project Library.
Uploading answers to students.

questions.text: What does ripple trimming help prevent?
choices: Unwanted gaps after shortening or extending edits.
Project settings.
Project Manager backups.
Using the Media page.

questions.text: Identify the term: a horizontal lane in the timeline that holds video or audio clips.
Identify the term: information about a clip such as shot, scene, take, comments, or technical details.
In one sentence, explain why an editor should organize footage before making detailed edits.

title: Q2 Quiz 2 Draft: Titles, Transitions, and Final Export

questions.text: What is a title used for in an edit?
choices: Readable text such as a title card, name label, or lower third.
A raw camera file.
A no-school calendar note.
A timeline backup folder.

questions.text: Which panel is commonly used to adjust title text, position, and selected effect settings?
choices: Inspector
Project Manager
Render Queue
Media Storage

questions.text: What is a keyframe?
choices: A point in time that stores a value so a property can change over time.
The final exported file.
A folder inside the Media Pool.
The first day of the grading period.

questions.text: When should a transition be used?
choices: When it supports the edit and does not distract from the story.
Between every single clip no matter what.
Only before importing footage.
Only after the final file is exported.

questions.text: What makes a lower third or title card classroom-ready?
choices: It is readable, spelled correctly, timed well, and supports the viewer.
It is as small as possible.
It covers the entire edit for the whole video.
It replaces the need to organize clips.

questions.text: Which page is used to render or export the finished timeline?
choices: Deliver
Media
Edit
Project Manager

questions.text: What is the render queue?
choices: A list of export jobs waiting to render.
A list of raw clips not imported.
A folder of quiz answers.
A set of A/B class periods.

questions.text: Why should the file name and save location be checked before rendering?
choices: So the exported file can be found and submitted correctly.
So the project deletes unused clips.
So the timeline automatically rewrites itself.
So the grading period changes.

questions.text: What is a codec?
choices: A method used to encode and compress video or audio data.
A title animation preset only.
A class join code.
A type of bin folder.

questions.text: Which export setting is commonly used for a web-friendly class submission?
choices: H.264 in an MP4 container.
A missing file path.
A project file with no rendered video.
A timeline with no title or final check.

questions.text: Why should an editor watch the final timeline before exporting?
choices: To catch gaps, unreadable titles, timing problems, or other mistakes.
To skip the render queue.
To delete the project manager.
To avoid saving the file.

questions.text: What should a simple motion effect do in a school edit?
choices: Guide attention or polish the communication without distracting.
Move randomly for the whole video.
Replace the need for a rough cut.
Hide all required evidence.

questions.text: Identify the term: a visual change used between two clips, such as a dissolve or wipe.
Identify the term: a list of export jobs waiting to be rendered.
Name two final quality checks an editor should make before exporting.
